segments.title: Modern warship growth and the unvirtuous circle
segments.summary: The lecture argues that modern warships keep growing because shrinking fleets push more sensors, weapons, magazine depth, and strategic slack onto each remaining hull.
segments.body: Smaller fleet numbers require each hull to do more, which means more sensors, weapons, and systems, which raises cost and invites further cuts. The lecture extends that logic through VLS magazine depth, containerized missile limits, construction pacing, maintenance reserves, and the need for mass as well as technology.

evidence.note: The opening frames weapons, sensors, geography, threats, risk, construction, and recurring warship size bands as the quick answer for why modern warships grow.
The transcript defines the unvirtuous circle: fewer hulls must carry more capability, become larger and more expensive, and then face further fleet-number cuts.
The VLS discussion argues that missile cells are the ship's practical magazine depth and that cutting cells after paying for sensors and combat systems creates capability gaps.
The construction and maintenance section links slow builds, reserve-force practice, dry-dock capacity, spares, and slack to fleet cost and operational availability.
The conclusion restates the cycle from shrinking fleets to more systems per ship, larger costs, and renewed fleet reductions.
